item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

At first when I got that it was the Trolley Problem I thought it would be just a cheap ethical experiment and probably downvote. But in the end it is a very interesting logical exercise using the Problem just as a device. Indeed, for me it highlights just how obtuse is the Trolley Problem and the two extreme takes on ethics it clashes.

For me the convoluted procedure justifies itself on the basis of reliability. Six can't say what happens if the dog is killed or if they kills themself. There is a need to keep things going if Six dies first. Also Six cannot guarantee their own follow-up of procedure if put under constant direct moral stress.

Deontology focus on action doesn't mean that it affords complete inaction, either. The Trolley problem puts 1 life against 5 because even for a deontologist it would be immoral to kill through inaction if the saving action had no moral consequence. More importantly, what's at stake isn't arguing how deontology works, but how the anomaly will interpret it, and then a less complex procedure would carry the risk of a breach.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not necessarily. The Trolley problem is very old, and on the academical field of Ethics it has been cliché for more than a hundred years, already.

What happens is that it is precisely a philosophical construct for academic purposes. It is a dilemma meant to throw arguments at it as thought experiments, not to actually inform anyone's actual morals.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No O5 voted, this is a ruse. And nothing happens to the person affected except a complete belief that they are 100% objectively bad.

Dr. Six is, indeed, affected all the time, but manages this. The problem is that if this thing gets out of control it won't be likely that enough personnel will stay able to fulfil their duties to the Foundation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That would violate deontological morality because, unlike himself, Dr. Six does not have total legal responsiblity over the animal.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Honestly I don’t see why Dr. Six cant do this alone, shocking himself and the dog occasionally. Despite this, I absolutely love it. When the dog and/or Dr. Six dies, is the anomaly neutralized, or is it now uncontainable since dead things can’t experience pain? +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I never even was aware about this "trolley dilemma" existing and was slightly boggled in the comment section by everybody talking about how cliche and passe it is.

My personal sense / awareness of ethics may be outdated / uncommon, it seems. Not sure what to think about that, but at least it makes me think.

item.title: Re: funny joke...............
item.content:encoded:
Imgur isn't loading for me. But is this by any chance related to some tracks? Perhaps…multiple tracks? And drifting of some sort?

EDIT: Finally got Imgur working. I called it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A very well written ethical dilemma. Didn't even know about deontology until know. Great work teaching me a hard concept.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The killer would become guilty of being immoral and effectively replace the dog's anomaly. The best case scenario is for the dog to die of natural causes so no one is at fault.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
very, VERY well written.
Although, could you clarify what happens to a person who is morally responsible (English inst my main language)?
and how are the O5's who voted (the 10 out of 13 that voted for yes) kept in check? don't they know about it to?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Gosh darnit why did you have to make it a dog? I love dogs and feel bad for the poor little thing.

Which…I guess is the point. In which case it's very effective. If it was just a human girl, well; one wouldn't care as much. But you had to make it a dog, which is much easier to empathise with than a human due to the desensitisation of humanity that the Foundation does. It's clever. Sad, but clever.

I have to give my dog a hug now :(

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Best SCP in it's series! Very beautiful work with the containment procedures. Also it is quite interesting to see a Keter that doesn't directly kill someone (or any SCP that doesn't directly kill someone). +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You did a wonderful job of cleaning this up. The tone is wonderful, some of the formatting needs a little work as it is a bit distracting, and it did make me squirm in my seat. Something I look forward to in an SCP.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I gave a -1, on the reasoning that it doesn't make much sense for a foundation-loyal researcher to infect an O5. O5 are supposed to be kept at a continent's length from SCPs at all times to avoid contamination. Keeping them rational and sane enough to make the hard decisions is the point.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Interesting, but why can't they just kill the dog?
I am withholding my vote for now. I may have to read it again.

item.title: funny joke...............
item.content:encoded:
you: "i_would_let_the_five_die.jpg"

me, an intellectual

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i have upvoted the poor ethics pup

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
*slow clap*

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I read 3133-A and 3133-B simultaneously, and so figured out pretty quickly that the containment procedures were meant to ensure that neither the -A nor the -B team were morally responsible for the pain, and I think the real version justifies it. I do feel sorry for the doctor (and O5-5), though…

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh no poor doggo

This is very well done. There might be simpler ways to contain this, but I like the two-fold procedures a lot. Also, the table view doesn't break on mobile, which is rare. +1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Downvoted. It's one thing to create an SCP that is meant to raise an ethical dilemma. This is an SCP that essentially turns to the audience and says, "Hello, I am an SCP that is meant to raise an ethical dilemma. Allow me to explain in detail the ethical dilemma I am raising, all the while pointing out that I am, once again, an ethical dilemma."

There are more interesting ways of raising a dilemma between deontological and consequentialist ethical stances than having the Foundation literally be attacked by deontological ethics while clip art of an actual trolley is displayed.

Also, what sort of tone were you going for with this article? Because making the threat too abstract and philosophical makes it not scary, and this is an SCP which goes a step further and makes the threat philosophy. It spells everything out, so there's no sense of mystery. It's not funny. It's not going for a tug at the heart strings. I don't know what I'm supposed to feel.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm conflicted with this…..it is very well executed but……..dogo………..

Sending abused fictional virtual dogo fictional virtual treats and pets.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is so similar to SCP-231, yet, it's not. Upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Both of these criticisms are totally fair. So far this has been among my more controversial SCPs in terms of votes and I'm guessing that most of the downvotes are for similar reasons to yours.

As for the first criticism, the trolley problem is absolutely a cliché, and I only included it because I thought it would make this easier to understand for more readers.

And for the second, I'm even thinking a lot of the same things; most of what I did in this respect was just because I thought it would be cool. The A and B versions of the document are a format screw idea that I had that I just really wanted to use, and I only chose an O5 over another ethicist because, again, I just liked the sound of it.

So, I mean, I have reasons for the choices I made, but I can see how not everyone would like them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I disagree that refusing to help SCP-3133 would be considered "inaction" given that multiple people must actively administer the shock, maintain the collar, feed the dog, and ensure proper containment. Even if the collar was set to automatically administer the shock, someone must check and maintain the collar, which would be the moral equivalent of pulling the lever. I also disagree that people who are aware of the SCP's true nature would be morally justified in directly harming it. Changing the morals of the Foundation does not directly harm anyone, it's the consequences that harm people, which, on a moral level, are out of the control of the individuals involved in 3133's containment. Sorry if that argument made no sense, but I wanted to express my viewpoint.

I love this SCP and the idea of an SCP changing the moral views of the people around it is a tough thing to describe. Morality is such an abstract concept, so the more specific you can make the SCP the easier it will be for people to understand.

+1 from me, keep it up

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have two issues with this article that prompted me to downvote.

The first is that I think the utilitarian/deontological trolley problem is bar none the most cliche ethical/philosophical "dilemma" in modern media today. The Ethics Committee Orientation and black white gray are both, in my view, the extent to which the trolley problem can be played straight in Foundation mythos; these are both, like, six years old, and now everything else has to be a play on it. This was, in my view, too much of just…the straightforward trolley problem.

Second, I couldn't really understand the logic of the plot. I don't get why there need to be two article formats: there are easier ways to contain the thing. People who are aware of the skip's true nature but do not act to prevent harm being done to it are not ethically responsible (under a deontological ethical framework but not a consequentialist one, ironically enough). So you can just have a team of researchers who know what the thing is, and only have one person responsible for giving the actual order. It's also still totally unclear to me why you would have to send this to an O5, who now has to seclude themselves in the middle of nowhere and commit regular self-harm, as opposed to another member of the ethics committee.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love everything about this, from the way format screw is used, to the inventive containment procedure, to the reminder that the foundation can't operate without moral relativism. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well written. I haven't understood much but I still enjoyed it. Good job. +1

item.title: Re: Author post
item.content:encoded: Now that I think about it, with a active d-class, He would try to remove it. As the effect makes the ethical code of the person around them change… this means containment would break… wow this is a deeper SCP then I thought, great job man! +1

item.title: Re: Author post
item.content:encoded:
Good question! A couple reasons:

1. A D-Class is almost definitely supervised by many people. The more people know about this SCP, the more people there are who are morally responsible for it, which is bad. An O5, though, is allowed to keep secrets.

2. An O5 is probably the most trustworthy person in the Foundation heirarchy for this sort of thing. A D-Class, or even a lower-level staff member, might be unreliable.

item.title: Re: Author post
item.content:encoded: If I might ask, I enjoyed this SCP alot. I'm just wondering in the death of Dr.Six, why would it be send to a 05 memeber. If the Dr dies at a horrible time, then this could go uncontained. Why not a D-Class?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
How interesting. What a neat, narratively justified format screw. It was refreshingly novel to see the first person used within the main body of a scip.

A little obtuse, but there's no easy way of making it clearer without fundamentally changing the subject matter.

An enthusiastic +1.

item.title: Author post
item.content:encoded:
My eighth SCP, and hopefully not too confusing.

Thanks to Baubius and Waterfire for feedback in chat! I also received offline feedback from peach time.
…

+ glossary/definitions
- glossary/definitions

Normative ethics: The ethical code that binds all rational agents. There is debate over what it is and if it exists.

Rational agent: In most cases, a person. More broadly, anyone or anything capable of consciously making a moral choice.

The Trolley Problem: A famous ethical dilemma in which you may (hypothetically) kill one person to save five.

Deontology/Deontological ethics: The belief that ethics come from duties, or rules. The consequences of a moral choice don't matter; instead, the choice itself does. A strict deontologist would allow the five to die in the Trolley Problem.

Consequentialism/Consequentialist ethics: The belief that ethics come from, well, the consequences of a moral choice. A strict consequentialist would kill the one in the Trolley Problem. The Foundation is, by and large, consequentialist. The most famous branch of consequentialist ethics is called Utilitarianism.


…
+ image attribution
- image attribution

The illustration of the Trolley Problem comes from Wikimedia Commons.

Image Source: https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/8/8c/Trolley_problem.png

Creator: McGeddon
